votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the logic of summing odd elements at even indices (0, 2, 4, ...) using a loop with a step of 2, which matches the recursive structure of the Coq function.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic by iterating over even indices and summing the elements that are odd.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic by iterating over even indices (0, 2, 4, ...) and summing the elements that are odd.

Both implementations sum odd numbers at even indices (0, 2, 4, ...). The Coq version uses recursion, processing elements at positions 0, 2, 4, ... by skipping pairs. The Python version iterates with step 2 starting at index 0. Both check oddness (Coq uses Z.odd, Python uses % 2 != 0) and accumulate the sum. They produce the same result for all concrete inputs.

The Python version iterates with step 2 starting at index 0. Both check oddness (Coq uses Z.odd, Python uses % 2 != 0) and accumulate matching values. They produce the same result for all concrete integer lists.
Both sum odd elements at even indices (0-based) and handle negatives consistently; Python’s % 2 behavior matches Coq’s Z.odd. The spec checks equality with the same function.
Both compute the sum of elements at even indices that are odd, handling negatives and all lengths identically.
Both sum elements at even indices (0-based) and include an element only if it is odd; Python’s % 2 behavior matches Coq’s Z.odd for all integers, including negatives.
